content.text: I need a reliable way to ensure that time slots are added to an 'IntervalTier' only if they don't conflict with existing entries.

To implement this, we first validate the type of the tier and then use the `check` flag to scan all existing intervals for any possible scheduling collisions before saving the new interval.

```python
def add_interval(self, begin, end, value, check=True):
        """Add an interval to the IntervalTier.

        :param float begin: Start time of the interval.
        :param float end: End time of the interval.
        :param str value: Text of the interval.
        :param bool check: Flag to check for overlap.
        :raises Exception: If overlap, begin > end or wrong tiertype.
        """
        if self.tier_type != 'IntervalTier':
            raise Exception('Tiertype must be IntervalTier')
        if check:
            if any(i for i in self.intervals if begin < i[1] and end > i[0]):
                raise Exception('No overlap is allowed')
            if begin > end:
                raise Exception('Begin must be smaller then end')
        self.intervals.append((begin, end, value))
```

The overlap check is strictly defined by `begin < i[1]` and `end > i[0]`, which means perfectly touching intervals (e.g., ending at 10 and starting at 10) are not considered overlaps and will be allowed.
Does the current logic allow two intervals to meet exactly at one boundary, like [0, 10] followed by [10, 20]?

Yes, this logic allows touching boundaries because the overlap condition strictly requires the start of one interval to be less than the end of another. The check would look like: 
```python
if begin < i[1] and end > i[0]: # If B_start < A_end AND B_end > A_start
    raise Exception('No overlap is allowed')
```
That makes sense, thanks for clarifying the boundary handling.
